nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich lagoon areas and shallow seaward reefs, typically at 3–30 m depth. Juveniles inhabit shallow seagrass beds and Acropora-rich reef flats; adults are generally found in pairs on shallow coral reefs.

diet.notes: Primarily an algae grazer. Marine algae sheets (nori) clipped to the rockwork at least three times weekly are generally recommended, supplemented with spirulina-based frozen foods, algae wafers, and vegetable-based flake. Modest amounts of mysis or krill have been documented as accepted but should not displace the algae-based staple.

summary: Indo-West Pacific rabbitfish with a yellow-gold body and bright blue spotting that intensifies with maturity. Generally peaceful and algae-grazing, with venomous dorsal and anal spines that should be handled with caution.
careNotes:
The Blue Spotted Rabbitfish is generally considered a moderately hardy choice for medium-to-large reef and FOWLR systems. Trade sources commonly cite adult sizes around 20 cm (8 in), while FishBase records a maximum length of 35 cm SL with a common length near 20 cm — the larger figure is more representative of fully grown wild specimens, so a long-term minimum aquarium volume on the order of 700 L is advisable (a spread observed across sources: LiveAquaria ~120 gal / 454 L, reefs.com ~125 gal / 473 L, ReefApp 1000 L / ~264 gal — biased upward per the tank-size precautionary rule).

Temperature: aquarium-trade sources generally advise 22–26°C (72–78°F); FishBase wild data extends to 27°C. The averaged husbandry range used here is 22–26°C, with a target near 24–25°C commonly cited.

Reef safety: generally considered reef-safe with caution. Well-fed specimens tend to leave corals alone, but underfed individuals have been documented nipping at LPS, soft corals, and macroalgae. Consistent algae feeding (nori three or more times weekly) is strongly advised.

Venomous spines: dorsal and anal fin spines are venomous and capable of delivering a painful sting. Handling should be done with a net or aquascaping tools — never with bare hands. Stings have been reported as rarely dangerous to humans but consistently painful.

Conspecific aggression: adults are generally intolerant of other rabbitfish (Siganidae) and foxfaces; only one specimen per tank is advised unless a confirmed mated pair is housed in a very large system. Juveniles in the wild have been documented forming small schools, with adults transitioning to pair living.

Color variation: when stressed, sleeping, or alarmed the body coloration may shift to a mottled, less vibrant pattern — this is normal camouflage behavior, not an illness sign.

Breeding: spawning has been documented in the wild but captive breeding in home aquariums has not been reliably reported.
breedingNotes: Wild spawning has been reported but captive breeding of Siganus corallinus in home aquariums has not been reliably documented and is generally considered impractical without specialized facilities.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary species page (collection URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/foxface-rabbitfish redirected to the product page). Confirmed Siganus corallinus, 8 in adult, 120 gal min, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, moderate care, peaceful, herbivore, reef-safe with caution, venomous.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Siganidae, Acanthuriformes), max size (35 cm SL, common 20 cm TL), depth 3–30 m, temperature 24–27°C, trophic level 2.0 (herbivore), Indo-West Pacific range. Size disagreement with trade sources (20 cm vs 35 cm): final range set to 20–35 cm to span both.

additional.name: Aquatics Unlimited
additional.notes: Confirmed ~8 in adult, 125 gal min, 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, moderate care, peaceful, herbivore, reef-safe with caution.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Independent care guide listing 1000 L (~264 gal) minimum tank for full-grown specimens and 35 cm adult size, supporting the upward-biased tank-size pick. Confirmed peaceful temperament, herbivore diet, pair-living, venomous spines.

fish.breedingNotes: Wild spawning has been documented around lunar cycles in pairs; captive breeding in home aquariums has not been reliably reported.
